figures.caption: Figure 1: Same Visual Signature, Different Algorithms. Visually, attention sinks appear identically as vertical stripes where multiple tokens attend to a single position. However, this pattern can implement two fundamentally different algorithms. (Left) Adaptive NOP: The sink acts as a suppression mechanism (“trash can”). Tokens attend here to effectively perform an identity operation and avoid updating their state. (Right) Broadcast: The sink acts as a communication hub (“coffee cup”). Tokens attend here to retrieve shared global information, actively writing a payload to the residual stream. We provide diagnostics to distinguish these regimes, showing that distinct interventions are required for each.

Figure 3: Broadcast sink signatures. A two-layer model trained on global broadcast learns a modular solution: (A) Layer 1 forms a broadcast hub while Layer 2 remains identity-like; (B) sink values retain content-scale norms; (C) query-key geometry selects the source token; and (D) the broadcast update is rank-1.

Figure 7: (A) High-entropy gating makes sinks appear faster. When the head is forced to perform a NOP for about half the tokens (maximal uncertainty about whether to update), optimization quickly discovers a dedicated sink position to reliably suppress updates. (B) Even an imperfect NOP pressure makes sinks appear. Decreasing the NOP factor γ pushes the desired output closer to zero on gated tokens, which increases the incentive to route to a null token and results in higher sink plateaus.
Table 2: ViT-Large architecture specifications for LeJEPA experiments.
Table 3: Optimization hyperparameters for LeJEPA experiments.
Table 5: Training data augmentation pipeline for LeJEPA experiments. Four augmented views are generated per image.
Table 8: Existing assets used in this work and their licenses or terms of use.

Figure 8: (Left) Registers absorb sink behavior. In DINOv2-G + Reg.(4), register tokens (pink) capture nearly all attention mass across layers, displacing patch and [CLS] sinks. (Right) Registers inherit both regimes. Register sinks cluster into the same two phenotypes: NOP (low norm, majority) and broadcast (high norm, rank-1). Registers are repurposed for both mechanisms.

Figure 9: Sink token transition across layers. EVA Giant and Clip OpenAI Large both exhibit a handoff pattern, although the pattern is more pronounced in EVA: [CLS] serves as the sink in early layers but yields to patch tokens in later layers.
Figure 10: Head specialization in EVA Giant. The entropy of values per layer shows that sink behavior is head-specific.
Figure 11: Head specialization in Clip OpenAI Large. The entropy of values per layer shows that sink behavior is head-specific.

Figure 12: Dual Phenomenology in EVA Giant. Sinks cluster into NOP sinks (low norm, bottom) and broadcast sinks (moderate to high norm, ≈rank-1 update, left). Both regimes coexist within the same model.

Figure 13: Dual Phenomenology in Clip OpenAI Large. Sinks cluster into NOP sinks (low norm, bottom) and broadcast sinks (moderate to high norm, ≈rank-1 update, left). Both regimes coexist within the same model.
Figure 14: Gating and registers mitigate high norms. Distribution of token norms across layers on the ImageNet-1k validation set.
